value.Title: GUEST COLUMN: CFU proud to provide reliable, affordable, sustainable power to customers

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJN-SXN3-S033-10VY-00000-00GUEST COLUMN: CFU proud to provide reliable, affordable, sustainable power to customers2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:17ZLexisNexisCOLUMNISTSGUEST COLUMN: CFU proud to provide reliable, affordable, sustainable power to customersSUSAN ABERNATHY

Cedar Falls Utilities has been providing affordable and reliable electric service to its customers for more than 100 years. We are proud to be a municipal utility with innovative, high-quality services.

A recent guest column in The Courier criticizing Cedar Falls Utilities was authored by an out-of-state special interest group funded by private donors. It is concerning when outside groups present a misleading picture of our community's energy use.

Cedar Falls residents deserve clear, accurate information about their energy. CFU is actively investing in the next generation of energy with an "all of the above" approach to our generation portfolio, focused on balancing sustainability, reliability and affordability.

Iowa is a national leader in renewable energy, and that success is something our customers should take pride in. More than 60% of the electricity delivered to our community comes from renewable resources through the regional grid operated by the Midcontinent Independent System Operator (MISO). Municipal utilities like CFU participate in a regional market because it allows communities to access cleaner and lower-cost energy. All utilities, including CFU, play a part in keeping the lights on across the grid through generation and transmission investments. Iowa's leadership in wind energy is a direct result of that collaboration.

Local generation continues to be important for our community. CFU must provide our community with both energy and capacity. Energy is purchased on the regional energy market through MISO. MISO enforces many rules and regulations to ensure grid stability and reliability. Capacity is also an important component. CFU must prove to MISO that it has enough capacity to serve its peak load at any given moment in time. Local generation provides crucial capacity for our community. The regional energy market is governed by the fundamentals of supply and demand. If we don't have enough capacity, we must purchase it from other utility providers at prices CFU cannot control. That could increase customers' utility bills significantly.

Wind and solar projects generate energy, but they contribute relatively little capacity value because their output depends on weather conditions and can be variable and difficult to predict. When the wind blows, there is abundant energy available for purchase in the regional energy market. However, when the wind is not blowing, fuel-based generation is critical to keeping the lights on. In addition, if there is a weather event that takes down transmission lines, CFU must have local generation to keep the power on. Our customers' health and safety cannot be placed solely in the hands of the regional electric grid. We need to have our own local solutions for reliability.

We are preparing for the future by maintaining critical generation and reinvesting in new, flexible power sources that can respond quickly to changes in renewable output and ensure power is available when it's needed most. CFU is advancing plans to build the new Viking Energy Center. Powered by highly efficient engines, the Viking Energy Center is designed to ramp up quickly when renewable generation dips, ensuring we can continue to maximize renewable use while maintaining reliable service. When the Viking Energy Center operates, it will not be displacing clean, renewable energy. It will be replacing generation from older, less efficient coal and natural gas fired plants.

At CFU, we understand that reliability is not optional. For that reason, we will continue to need fuel-based generation for the foreseeable future. The Viking Energy Center replaces existing gas generating units that are more than 50 years old with new, highly efficient dual fuel units that are both reliable and affordable.

Iowa is a renewable energy success story, and Cedar Falls is proud to be part of that momentum. We remain committed to partnering with our customers to grow energy efficiency programs and support smart conservation choices that reduce overall demand. CFU will continue supporting a cleaner energy future while ensuring the reliable, affordable service our community expects.

CFU's customers can be assured that when the larger regional grid is under stress and all our neighboring communities are in the dark, Cedar Falls will be a beacon of light because we invested in local, efficient, reliable fuel-based generation.

value.Title: Cedar Falls electricity isn't as clean it seems

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGN-KCJ3-S5WT-92P8-00000-00Cedar Falls electricity isn't as clean it seems2026-05-02T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:17ZLexisNexisWCFCOURIER.COMCedar Falls electricity isn't as clean it seemsStaff Writer

Cedar Falls takes pride in making smart energy choices for its community. On the surface, there's much to celebrate. Cedar Falls Utilities, the city-owned municipal utility, reports that 61% of the energy used in Cedar Falls was generated by renewable resources.

But when you take a careful look under the hood, things are not as clean as the numbers suggest. According to CFU's own 2026 budget, 99% of the electricity generation owned by Cedar Falls still comes from fossil fuels – mainly coal, natural gas, and oil. Notably, CFU owns more coal generation than any other municipal utility in Iowa, and over 30% of the power it owns is in minority shares of MidAmerican Energy's coal plants in the state. CFU currently has no plans to retire these fossil-fuel assets or replace them with safer, cleaner alternatives, even though the city has committed to reaching carbon-neutrality by 2050.

This isn't about blaming CFU or its leadership. It's about understanding what "clean energy" really means and why it's so important to read the fine print.

CFU claims that most of the energy it purchases is from the regional power market and that 61% of the energy produced by the regional power market and distributed in Cedar Falls comes from renewable resources. So, what is the regional power market and where does that "61% renewable" number come from?

The regional power grid CFU is referring to is operated by MISO, the Midcontinent Independent System Operator.

The regional energy market MISO is like a large bucket of water. That bucket is filled by utilities and energy producers across the region some pouring in clean water (from renewables like wind and solar), and others pouring in dirty water (from coal, gas, and oil).

Cedar Falls draws its electricity from this shared bucket across Iowa, which happens to be about 61% renewable today. In fact, Iowa produces the fourth most total renewable energy of any state in the nation. This is something to be proud of – the perfect example of how local resources can fuel a cleaner, growing economy.

Unfortunately, this means CFU is actually lagging far behind the state and region's success with clean energy. In addition to its existing owned fossil fuel generation, CFU is currently proposing a controversial natural gas power plant in Cedar Falls. Rather than having a robust, public process to evaluate and identify future energy generation needs for the community, the process for moving forward with this natural gas plant was done with little transparency and public input.

Luckily, Cedar Falls benefits from having a city-owned utility, which gives us far more control over electricity generation than most other communities. This means Cedar Falls can more easily invest in clean energy projects that are cheaper and faster to build than fossil fuel projects. CFU can also adopt smart technology like smart thermostats and programs that encourage people to use less energy when the grid is stressed. To make it easier to participate, utilities often off er incentives such as solar groupbuys, on-bill financing, and rebates that help more residents to take control of their own energy use.

CFU does operate an energy efficiency program and supports customer-owned solar and wind installations. These are important tools, but they are not enough.

The question of whether Cedar Falls will lead or lag on this transition is one this community deserves to ask openly and answer together.

Cedar Falls is not alone in confronting this gap. Across Iowa, aging coal plants continue to operate well past their prime, carrying real costs for public health, the environment, and ratepayers costs that rarely appear in a utility's headline numbers. "Iowa's Dirty Secret," a new documentary screening at 6:30 p.m. Wednesday, May 6 at the Cedar Falls Public Library, takes a deeper look. For anyone trying to understand what's really powering their home, and what it would take to change it, it's essential viewing. Following the film, a panel of experts will answer your questions on clean energy and fossil fuel pollution.

Raina Genaw of Cedar Falls is a campaign organizing intern with the Environmental Law & Policy Center.

value.Title: Letter: Urging people to oppose CIPCO plant, turn to solar instead

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGS-2B83-RSPH-F04K-00000-00Letter: Urging people to oppose CIPCO plant, turn to solar instead2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:17ZLexisNexisLETTERSLetter: Urging people to oppose CIPCO plant, turn to solar insteadStaff Writer

The Scott County Board of Supervisors voted unanimously to deny CIPCO a zoning change to build a natural gas power plant by Maysville. But CIPCO would not take no for an answer and petitioned the Iowa Utilities Commission to build it. I urge everyone to go online at efs.iowa.gov and submit an objection to docket #GCU-2026-1.

CIPCO said it needs this plant to work sporadically 15-20% of the time to cover energy needs in peak seasons. These peaking plants cause more pollution than the typical power plant because they turn on and off frequently. Our energy needs could be met by employing more renewable energy.

Solar farms could be installed on agricultural lands. Solar arrays allow the land to get more fertile the more years they're in use. Crops or wildflowers could be planted in conjunction with the arrays. Cattle could graze on the site also.

This could be another egg in a farmer's basket to provide income in these unstable times. Iowa needs renewable energy to get a handle on the pollution that is causing our high cancer rates.

There is an obstacle to building these solar arrays. The board of supervisors has a code in place to restrict building these arrays on farmland. Many farms have their own solar. I would rather have solar on farmland, than a big polluting generating plant with all of its acres of concrete.

Let the board know to revisit this code and go with renewable energy to cover our increasing energy needs.

Mary Maher

Davenport

value.Title: Urging people to oppose CIPCO plant, turn to solar instead

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGR-K873-S207-M1YT-00000-00Urging people to oppose CIPCO plant, turn to solar instead2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:17ZLexisNexisNEWSUrging people to oppose CIPCO plant, turn to solar insteadStaff Writer

The Scott County Board of Supervisors voted unanimously to deny CIPCO a zoning change to build a natural gas power plant by Maysville. But CIPCO would not take no for an answer and petitioned the Iowa Utilities Commission to build it. I urge everyone to go online at efs.iowa. gov and submit an objection to docket #GCU-2026-1.

CIPCO said it needs this plant to work sporadically 15-20% of the time to cover energy needs in peak seasons. These peaking plants cause more pollution than the typical power plant because they turn on and off frequently. Our energy needs could be met by employing more renewable energy.

Solar farms could be installed on agricultural lands. Solar arrays allow the land to get more fertile the more years they're in use. Crops or wildflowers could be planted in conjunction with the arrays. Cattle could graze on the site also.

This could be another egg in a farmer's basket to provide income in these unstable times. Iowa needs renewable energy to get a handle on the pollution that is causing our high cancer rates.

There is an obstacle to building these solar arrays. The board of supervisors has a code in place to restrict building these arrays on farmland. Many farms have their own solar. I would rather have solar on farmland, than a big polluting generating plant with all of its acres of concrete.

Let the board know to revisit this code and go with renewable energy to cover our increasing energy needs.

Mary Maher Davenport

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-F6G3-RRRH-Y069-00000-00Supervisor candidate: Dennis Willhoit2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:17ZLexisNexisSTATE AND REGIONAL NEWSSupervisor candidate: Dennis WillhoitCHAD DRURYOttumwa Courier, Iowa

Name: Dennis Willhoit

Age: 62

Political party: Democrat

Hometown: RuralWapello County

Current occupation: Executive Artistic Director, American Gothic Performing Arts; owner, Appanoose Rapids Mercantile, 1933 and The Short Stay; family farm manager

College degree: B.M., Simpson College; M.M., University of Massachusetts Amherst; D.M.A., University of Iowa

Why are you seeking election, and what do you hope to accomplish during your term? "I'm seeking election to help move Wapello County forward with a broader, more resilient tax base. We need to think creatively about growth, supporting responsible renewable energy, strengthening small business and attracting new investment — while respecting property rights and fiscal responsibility ... I also believe arts and culture are essential economic drivers; vibrant communities attract people, jobs and opportunity ... During my term, I will focus on practical solutions that increase revenue without overburdening taxpayers, improve transparency, and position our county for long-term stability and growth."

For many years, Wapello County's budget has been tight for various reasons, some out of the county's control. What do you think the county can do — or should be doing — to increase revenue? "Wapello County needs to broaden and stabilize its tax base rather than rely on the same sources year after year. That starts with supporting responsible growth: renewable energy projects, small business expansion, housing development, and targeted industry recruitment that fits our workforce and infrastructure. These are opportunities to bring in new revenue without placing additional strain on existing taxpayers ... We should also be more proactive in pursuing state, federal and private grant funding, and in partnering regionally to compete for larger opportunities. Strengthening tourism and quality-of-life assets — especially arts, culture and recreation — helps attract visitors, residents, and investment. Economic vitality follows cultural vitality ... At the same time, we must ensure consistency and predictability in our local policies. When rules shift or become barriers to development, we risk losing investment to other counties or states. Finally, ongoing review of county operations for efficiency and transparency ensures that every dollar is used wisely. Growing revenue and managing resources responsibly must go hand in hand."

Why should businesses and families come to, and invest in, the county, and what does "open for business" look like to you? "Wapello County has strong fundamentals: a hard-working workforce, a good location, and a community that values quality of life. We should be telling that story clearly — and backing it up with action ... To me, 'open for business' means predictable, transparent policies and a willingness to partner. Businesses and families need to know what to expect: clear timelines, consistent regulations, and local leadership focused on solutions, not barriers. it also means investing in infrastructure, housing, and workforce development so growth is sustainable ... We also need to be honest about costs. Property taxes are a concern, and that's exactly why expanding the tax base is so important — so we can relieve pressure over time while maintaining essential services. Just as important is community vitality. Arts, culture, recreation, and strong schools are essential to attracting and retaining people. When a place is vibrant, people want to live there — and investment follows."

Solar farms could be installed on agricultural lands. Solar arrays allow the land to get more fertile the more years they're in use. Crops or wildflowers could be planted in conjunction with the arrays. Cattle could graze on the site also.

This could be another egg in a farmer's basket to provide income in these unstable times. Iowa needs renewable energy to get a handle on the pollution that is causing our high cancer rates.

There is an obstacle to building these solar arrays. The board of supervisors has a code in place to restrict building these arrays on farmland. Many farms have their own solar. I would rather have solar on farmland, than a big polluting generating plant with all of its acres of concrete.

Let the board know to revisit this code and go with renewable energy to cover our increasing energy needs.

Mary Maher

Davenport

urn:contentItem:6JG9-M713-S0TV-6193-00000-00The Rewind: May 12026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:34ZLexisNexisREWINDThe Rewind: May 1Staff Writer

In case you missed it: Forest City wildlife tracking tower dedicated to young birder

The Winnebago County Conservation Board dedicated a Motus wildlife tracking tower at Hanson Nature Center in honor of Mae Helgeson, an 11-year-old birder who died in an accident. The tower, part of the international Motus Wildlife Tracking System, monitors birds, bats, and butterflies in north-central Iowa, contributing to global migration research.

The Helgeson family funded the tower through memorial donations, transforming their grief into a public resource. The tower offers educational opportunities with interactive displays and Mae's photography. It fills a geographic gap in the tracking network, enhancing understanding of migratory species' routes and behaviors.

Read full article

Alaskan adventure: Osage teacher goes north for the Iditarod dog sled race

Kari Wright, a third-grade teacher at Lincoln Elementary in Osage, experienced the Iditarod as the 2026 Teacher on the Trail in March. She flew in a bush plane, met mushers, and blogged about the race, connecting it to classroom lessons. Wright has used the Iditarod for educational activities for 19 years, inspiring students in subjects like math and science.

Despite falling ill and missing part of the race, Wright recovered to witness the finish in Nome. Her students engaged with her journey via FaceTime, enhancing their learning experience. Former students expressed excitement, recalling their past Iditarod activities.

Read full article

Cerro Gordo County officials clash with state over solar development

The Iowa Utilities Commission advanced the review of River City Energy LLC's proposed 500-megawatt solar project in Cerro Gordo County, despite local opposition. The commission's April 27 order requires more information from the company, while the county has enacted a ban on such developments after a temporary moratorium.

The state commission's decision highlights the tension between state and local authorities over energy infrastructure. While Iowa law supports renewable energy growth, Cerro Gordo County officials argue these projects conflict with local land-use priorities. The commission has yet to decide on the generating certificate, with potential impacts on future renewable projects in rural areas.

Read full article

In case you missed it: Veteran bull rider from Brazil competes in Mason City event

Pedro Fonesca, a 32-year-old Brazilian native now living in Elk River, Minnesota, balances running a construction business with bull riding. A U.S. citizen for a year, Fonesca won the 2025 Rice Rodeo Tour bull riding championship and recently competed in Mason City. He began riding bulls at 15 in Brazil and moved to the U.S. at 25 to compete in professional events.

Fonesca, who stays close to home for competitions, appreciates the adrenaline and personal challenge of bull riding. His brother Joao also rides bulls in the U.S. despite past injuries. The Rice Rodeo Tour will visit Worth and Mitchell County fairs this summer.

Read full article

In case you missed it: North Iowa historical museum is being refreshed with some new exhibits

Mary Loden, appointed director of the Kinney Pioneer Museum in June 2025, is spearheading efforts to revitalize the museum near Mason City. Loden aims to enhance visibility from Highway 122 through landscaping and reduce interior clutter. She is developing new exhibits, including one on John Dillinger's 1934 bank robbery and another on John Kopecky, founder of the North Iowa Band Festival.

The museum will host several events in 2026, starting with a vintage tractor ride on May 9-10. Other events include a car show on July 18 and the Apple Cider and Donut Day on Sept. 27. Loden seeks more volunteers and board members to support the museum's efforts.

urn:contentItem:6JGB-XYT3-SH69-R3JY-00000-00The Rewind: May 12026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:17ZLexisNexisREWINDThe Rewind: May 1Staff Writer

The BLK Squirrel celebrating 10 years of good drinks, community on the 100 Block

The BLK Squirrel bar, located at 154 W. Broadway in Council Bluffs, offers a diverse range of drinks from Busch Light to fine bourbon. Owned by Andy Mead and Nathan Verzal, the bar has been a neighborhood staple since 2016. The space features a welcoming atmosphere with exposed brick and vintage decor, aiming to be a comfortable spot for all visitors.

Mead and Verzal, experienced bartenders, also operate The Green Room in Omaha. They emphasize community and cooperation among local businesses, contributing to the vibrant 100 Block district. The bar is open daily, providing a space where diverse clientele can connect over drinks and conversation.

Read full article →

Mary Ann Hanusa may draw controversy just not for her role in politics

Mary Ann Hanusa, currently the Pottawattamie County auditor, has a long history in public service, including roles in the National Archives and under Presidents Reagan and Bush. She also managed Sen. Chuck Grassley's campaign and served in the Iowa House. Hanusa, passionate about history, emphasizes its narrative aspect over memorization.

Raised in Council Bluffs, Hanusa credits her parents and history teachers for inspiring her career. Outside of work, she enjoys cooking and baking, viewing it as therapeutic. Hanusa, who listens to Christmas music year-round, aims to be remembered for her kindness and convictions.

Read full article →

Jeff Jorgensen faces reelection for first time since arrest in 2024 and grief counseling

Jeff Jorgensen seeks a second term on the Pottawattamie County Board of Supervisors, despite a 2024 arrest for driving while intoxicated. He attributed the incident to grief over his wife's death, completed a diversion program, and paid a fine. Jorgensen aims to foster county growth and address tax issues, emphasizing collaboration with cities like Council Bluffs and Omaha.

Jorgensen opposes new windmills due to local sentiment and advocates for selling or leasing the county-owned Crescent Hill ski hill. He stresses the board's need to manage budgets effectively, especially concerning infrastructure and salaries, while remaining open to community input.

Read full article →

Charlie Johnson hopes to join Board of Supervisors after falling short in last election

Charlie Johnson, a Republican, is running for the Pottawattamie County Board of Supervisors after previous unsuccessful attempts. Following Scott Belt's removal, Johnson was not appointed last year, but he has since focused on understanding county operations. He aims to bring stable leadership, accountability, and transparency to the board, emphasizing listening to residents and fostering cooperation with local communities.

Johnson identifies taxes and budget management as major concerns, advocating for innovative solutions to reduce taxpayer burdens. He supports collaboration with cities and prioritizes infrastructure improvements and renewable energy regulation, while respecting farmers' rights. Johnson's diverse experience in public, private and nonprofit sectors bolsters his candidacy.

Read full article →

urn:contentItem:6JHV-RSJ3-S0JT-N153-00000-00Researchers from University of Oklahoma Describe Findings in Renewable Energy (The Role of Environmental NGOs in the Renewable Energy-Environmental Interface)2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:17ZLexisNexisENERGY - RENEWABLE ENERGYResearchers from University of Oklahoma Describe Findings in Renewable Energy (The Role of Environmental NGOs in the Renewable Energy-Environmental Interface)


2026 MAY 08 (NewsRx) -- By a News Reporter-Staff News Editor at Energy Daily News -- Researchers detail new data in renewable energy. According to news reporting from Norman, Oklahoma, by NewsRx journalists, research stated, "Nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) play an important role in the interface between business, government and society, including serving as a link between diverse stakeholders, amplifying public visibility, and serving as a watch dog."


Our news editors obtained a quote from the research from University of Oklahoma: "This research seeks to understand the involvement and experience of environmental NGO (ENGO) staff members in the environmental planning of utility-scale wind and solar projects. We conducted 19 one-hour interviews with individuals representing 13 ENGOs which were located in or had projects within North Dakota, South Dakota, Nebraska, Kansas, Iowa, Oklahoma, and Texas. We found that, overall, engagement with the renewable energy industry was mixed, with some organizations being very involved and others having limited to no engagement. Participants also shared positive as well as more challenging engagement experiences they have had."
According to the news editors, the research concluded: "Overall, ENGOs see a number of potential opportunities to engage more in renewable energy planning, particularly in collaboration with renewable energy developers, to move renewable energy deployment forward while balancing land use and environmental concerns."
For more information on this research see: The Role of Environmental NGOs in the Renewable Energy-Environmental Interface. Land, 2026,15(4):684. (Land - http://www.mdpi.com/journal/land). The publisher for Land is MDPI AG.
A free version of this journal article is available at https://doi.org/10.3390/land15040684.
Our news journalists report that additional information may be obtained by contacting Claire Burch, Department of Geography and Environmental Sustainability, University of Oklahoma, Norman, OK 73019-1007, United States. Additional authors for this research include Rebecca Loraamm.
Keywords for this news article include: University of Oklahoma, Norman, Oklahoma, United States, North and Central America, Energy, Oil & Gas, Renewable Energy.

value.Title: Turkish Companies Eye U.S. Expansion at SelectUSA Summit

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-04X3-RS19-62MY-00000-00Turkish Companies Eye U.S. Expansion at SelectUSA Summit2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:47ZLexisNexisTurkish Companies Eye U.S. Expansion at SelectUSA Summit

Link to Image

More than 30 Turkish companies descended on Maryland this week to scout investment prospects spanning renewable energy, robotics, health care technology, and dental services at the SelectUSA Investment Summit, held May 3–6 near Washington, D.C.



The annual summit drew thousands of participants — including investors, economic development officials, and sector specialists from over 100 countries.

Tarik Sarvan, chairman of solar technology firm CW Enerji, said his company currently exports between $5 and $6 million in products monthly from Türkiye to the U.S. and is evaluating investment opportunities on a state-by-state basis. "We are trying to create a gateway where we can sell our products," he said, noting that manufacturing would stay in Türkiye while any U.S. presence would center on assembly and distribution.

Bakit Baydaliev, CEO of DOF Robotics, said the U.S. already represents 40 percent of the firm's revenues and is climbing. The company intends to scale its immersive entertainment offerings across several states, including Montana, Wyoming, and Colorado.

RealWorks Product and Marketing Director Fulya Bayram said the company is focused on expanding its AI-driven health care platform HaloScape in the U.S. and deepening relationships with state authorities and technology giants including Nvidia. "Our goal this year is to slightly increase our market share in the US, strengthen Türkiye's representation here, and build close relationships with the states," she said.

Dental startup dentalPrices and software firm IWRobotx also participated, both identifying the U.S. as central to their international expansion ambitions. Dr. Efe Celebi, founder of dental chain Dentgroup, said dentalPrices has already launched operations in Budapest and plans to enter the U.S. market before year-end.

Juliet Abdel, president of the Cedar Rapids Metro Economic Alliance, highlighted her region's competitive advantages for foreign investors — including lower operating costs, affordable living, and multimodal logistics infrastructure, with particular growth potential in aerospace, avionics, and the bioeconomy. "The industries which really overlap with our interests were energy, defense and manufacturing…I'll continue following up on the opportunities that came up in our one-on-one meetings," she said.

Chris Chung, CEO of The Economic Development Partnership of North Carolina, praised Turkish companies as "globally minded," adding: "Türkiye is a big market in and of itself, but there's a lot of opportunities sitting outside of Türkiye, including to both sell and produce products in the United States, and that's where the intersection of opportunity sits for us."

urn:contentItem:6JHM-RY53-RV86-D51P-00000-00Flex Ltd. (NASDAQ:FLEX) Posts Acquisition of EP to Expand Critical Power Platform2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:17ZLexisNexisFlex Ltd. (NASDAQ:FLEX) Posts Acquisition of EP to Expand Critical Power Platform

AMERICAN DAILY STOCK REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

06 May 2026 14:16 EDT

Flex Ltd. (NASDAQ:FLEX) has completed its acquisition of Electrical Power Products Inc. (EP²), a U.S.-based provider of engineered-to-order electrical power control and protection systems, strengthening its position in critical infrastructure markets including utilities, power generation, and data centers. The acquisition was finalized on 6th May 2026 and expands Flex's Critical Power portfolio by adding EP²'s engineering and manufacturing capabilities in customized power control systems.

EP², headquartered in Des Moines, Iowa, brings over 35 years of experience designing and manufacturing control and relay panels, as well as integrated modular control buildings used in utility and industrial applications. These systems are deployed in environments requiring high-reliability electrical protection and control, aligning with Flex's existing capabilities.

Flex's Chief Executive Officer Revathi Advaithi said the acquisition strengthens the company's Critical Power platform and aligns with its broader strategy to support rising demand for resilient electrical infrastructure. EP²'s customer-focused operating model and long-standing utility and industrial client relationships will enable Flex to deliver customized power solutions at scale. Following the acquisition, EP² will be integrated into Flex's Embedded and Critical Power segment, retaining its leadership team and workforce while aligning with Flex's global operations, and the move is expected to broaden Flex's footprint in the U.S. industrial manufacturing sector, particularly in the Midwest, as a technology partner for customers facing increased power demand and infrastructure complexity.

The company specializes in electronics manufacturing services (EMS), providing end-to-end solutions for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) in sectors such as automotive, healthcare, consumer electronics, and industrial technology. Its core activities include product design, supply chain optimization, and sustainable production processes, enabling clients to innovate and scale efficiently. Among its fastest-growing segments are electric vehicle components and renewable energy solutions, fueled by rising demand for eco-friendly technologies, as well as advanced medical devices driven by healthcare digitization. With a strong global presence and emphasis on operational excellence, Flex continues to adapt to market dynamics, delivering high-value, customized solutions that support technological advancements across industries. urn:contentItem:6JHK-G233-S00J-H4WG-00000-00PepsiCo inks new certificate-backed ammonia supply deal2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:51:17ZLexisNexisNEWSPepsiCo inks new certificate-backed ammonia supply dealLizzy Lancaster

Global food and beverage manufacturer PepsiCo will collaborate with firms TalusAg and S3 markets to develop a low-carbon ammonia certification system which could help to decarbonise the wider fertilizer and agriculture supply chain. The collaboration also includes...

Global food and beverage manufacturer PepsiCo will collaborate with firms TalusAg and S3 markets to develop a low-carbon ammonia certification system which could help to decarbonise the wider fertilizer and agriculture supply chain. The collaboration also ...

Global food and beverage manufacturer PepsiCo will collaborate with firms TalusAg and S3 markets to develop a low-carbon ammonia certification system which could help to decarbonise the wider fertilizer and agriculture supply chain. The collaboration also includes a supply agreement between PepsiCo and TalusAg for 30,000t low-carbon ammonia with an option to increase volumes to a total of 71,000t over an unspecified time frame, the firms said. Agricultural technology firm TalusAg has developed a small modular ammonia system which uses renewable power to produce ammonia at or near the point of use. The firm has a 1,000 t/yr renewable ammonia pilot project in Boone, Iowa already in operation. The firm has a second project planned in Eagle Grove, Iowa, which will produce around 7,000 t/yr. Both sites are strategically located within the US' Corn Belt and will supply local farmers for direct ammonia application. TalusAg is also working with Australian developer Minbos on a renewable ammonia project in Angola. TalusAg plans to generate environmental attribute certificates (EACs) from its Boone project via S3 markets. S3 markets is an EAC provider for low-carbon commodities operating with verified blockchain technology. Together with PepsiCo, the firms hope to develop a tradeable market for EACs of low-carbon ammonia with a book-and-claim chain of custody model, similar to the way in which renewable electricity certificates operate and can be traded. A book-and-claim certification system for ammonia would allow end users of agricultural products like PepsiCo to address the emissions in their supply chains through the purchase of EACs for low-carbon fertilizers, thereby supporting production costs but without forcing farmers to pay a premium they cannot afford. The initial agreement will cover PepsiCo's European, sub-Saharan Africa, Asia-Pacific and global teams, the firm said. PepsiCo also signed an agreement last month with US fertilizer producer CF industries for the supply of low-carbon urea ammonium nitrate fertilizer (UAN). Similar initiatives are already underway to develop EACs for ammonia, reflecting the industry's growing need to find a way to bypass farmers' inability to pay increased costs for low-carbon fertilizers. These initiatives include the Low Carbon Fertilizer Alliance, of which ammonia producer CF is a founding member, and the Ammonia Energy Association's pilot book-and-claim certification system which launched last year. By Lizzy Lancaster

Turkish businesspeople came together with representatives from U.S. states during the SelectUSA Investment Summit this week to explore investment opportunities across sectors ranging from renewable energy and robotics to health care technology and dental s...

Turkish businesspeople came together with representatives from U.S. states during the SelectUSA Investment Summit this week to explore investment opportunities across sectors ranging from renewable energy and robotics to health care technology and dental services. The summit, held on May 3-6 in Maryland near Washington, brought together thousands of participants, including investors, economic development officials and industry experts from over 100 countries. More than 30 Turkish companies attended the event. Tarık Sarvan, chairperson of leading Turkish solar technology company CW Enerji, said the firm exports around $5 million-$6 million worth of products monthly from Türkiye to the U.S. and is considering investment opportunities on a state-by-state basis. "We are trying to create a gateway where we can sell our products," he said, stressing that production would remain in Türkiye while potential U.S. operations would focus on assembly and sales. Bakit Baydaliev, CEO of technology firm DOF Robotics, said the U.S. already accounts for 40% of the company’s market and that the share is growing steadily. The company plans to expand its immersive entertainment businesses across multiple U.S. states, including in Montana, Wyoming and Colorado. RealWorks Product and Marketing Director Fulya Bayram said the company aims to expand the U.S. footprint of its AI-powered health care platform HaloScape and strengthen ties with state officials and major firms such as Nvidia. "Our goal this year is to slightly increase our market share in the U.S., strengthen Türkiye’s representation here and build close relationships with the states,” she said. Other Turkish firms attending the summit included dental technology startup dentalPrices and software company IWRobotx, both of which said they view the US market as key to global expansion. Dr. Efe Çelebi, founder of the dental clinic chain Dentgroup, said his company dentalPrices has expanded into Europe with operations in Budapest and is now planning to enter the U.S. market by the end of the year. Juliet Abdel, president of the Cedar Rapids Metro Economic Alliance, said the company offers foreign investors lower business costs, affordable living and strong transportation access as a Midwestern hub, with growth potential in aerospace and avionics, the bioeconomy and technology. "The industries which really overlap with our interests were energy, defense and manufacturing ... I'll continue following up on the opportunities that came up in our one-on-one meetings," she said about her meetings with investors. Chris Chung, CEO of The Economic Development Partnership of North Carolina, described Turkish companies as "globally minded." "Türkiye is a big market in and of itself, but there's a lot of opportunities sitting outside of Türkiye, including to both sell and produce products in the United States, and that's where the intersection of opportunity sits for us," he said.
